questions.prompt: Order the gTLD lifecycle stages from active to permanently lost.
questions.explanation: Active to grace to redemption to pending delete to deleted. Roughly 65 days from expiry to permanent loss for a typical gTLD.

questions.prompt: A client says their domain 'auto-renews automatically every year, so it can't have expired.' WHOIS shows `pendingDelete` and the card on file expired four months ago. What's the calibrated answer?
choices: Auto-renew failed silently. The domain is past redemption in pendingDelete; recovery isn't available.

choices.label: Auto-renew always succeeds; tell the client there's a registrar bug and escalate to support.
choices.rationale: Auto-renew is best-effort. It fails silently when the card declines or expires.

choices.label: Wait a few days; pendingDelete sometimes lifts automatically.
choices.rationale: pendingDelete is a one-way stage. It doesn't lift. The domain drops to the open market.

choices.label: Pay the standard renewal fee through the registrar panel to restore it immediately.
choices.rationale: Standard renewal only works during the grace period. pendingDelete is past that window.

questions.explanation: Auto-renew is best-effort; it fails silently if the card on file declines or expires. By the time the domain reaches pendingDelete, it has passed grace (~30 days) and redemption (~30 days, expensive but recoverable). Recovery at pendingDelete isn't available. The conversation now is whether to watch for the name to drop back to the public market.

questions.prompt: Auto-renew is a guarantee: as long as it's enabled, the domain will renew automatically every cycle.
questions.explanation: Auto-renew tries the card on file. When the card has expired, been declined, or been changed without updating the registrar, auto-renew fails (often silently) and the domain enters grace. Renewal-reminder emails and expiry monitoring are what prevent silent failures.
